dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Hemorrhage/Lacerations.
Hi, my name is Sarah, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I had some rectal bleeding and felt lightheaded.
When did the bleeding start today?
It began around 1 p.m.
Can you state your name for me, please?
My name is Patricia.
Are you feeling awake and alert right now?
Yes, I am aware and alert.
Can you open your eyes and move your head a bit?
I can manage that easily.
What city are we in right now, do you know?
This is Richmond.
Can you clamp my fingers with your left hand, please?
I can do that here.
Do you have any trouble speaking or swallowing?
Everything is fine on that front. No issues there.
How is your breathing at the moment? Is it steady?
It remains steady and normal.
The respiratory rate is at 13, steady and regular.
Are you short of breath or experiencing any discomfort from breathing?
Not at all, I'm breathing normally.
We're checking for pulses. Where can you feel a pulse right now?
Radial pulse is present, but it's weak.
The pulse is measured at 85, but it's stable.
Are you experiencing any pain at this time? What about numbness or tingling?
I don't have any pain or numbness.
Current blood pressure is 57 over 26.

Are there any other symptoms you'd like to share besides the bleeding and lightheadedness? 30. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: Yes, I've also had some episodes of diarrhea.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What caused the rectal bleeding today?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I took a laxative earlier in the morning before dialysis. 33. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: How would you describe the stool look or color after the incident? 34. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: It was very dark and tarry, with a strong odor.
On a pain scale from 0 to 10, where would you place the discomfort you're experiencing now? 36. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: severity; Patient: The level of pain is at 0 for me right now.

Jaundice, pallor, etc.; EMT: What is the color and feel of your skin at the moment? 38. Secondary Assessment; skin exam: Color: Jaundice, pallor, etc.; Patient: My skin appears normal, but my hands are cold.
The current glucose level is at 127, which is stable.
Oxygen saturation is at 26% now, though it improved when my hands were warmed.
EKG readings indicate sinus tachycardia.
We've completed and placed the four-lead ECG monitoring.
Based on the readings, we'll follow protocol 3-13 for rectal bleeding and hypotension.
Attempting IV access on your left arm.
The first IV insertion was not successful.
Now trying your right arm for the second attempt.
That didn't work either, the second IV attempt failed.
One more attempt for IV access will be made, in a different location.

Glasgow Coma Score is 15. Eye, verbal, and motor functions are all appropriate. 50. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; EMT: Could you please tell me about your medical history? 51. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Bystander: She has a history of diabetes, COPD, heart failure, and is on dialysis. Also, she has a previous heart attack and a lower leg amputation. 52. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; EMT: What medications are you currently taking, if any? 53. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Partner: She's on insulin for diabetes, and she uses medications related to dialysis. She has known allergies to Percocet, fentanyl, warfarin, morphine, and Niacin / Simvastatin, plus naltrexone.
A 12-lead EKG was performed, showing nonspecific intraventricular conduction delay and minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy.
There are also occasional ventricular premature complexes and possible lateral ischemia as per the ST/T-wave changes recorded.
Rechecking respiration rate which is now at 16 and depth is normal.
Blood pressure has slightly drifted to 66 over 46.
Pulmonary reading is stable again at 77 beats per minute.
Glucose is undetectable at this time.
The SpO2 remains at 0 with no improvement observed even after warming.
The EKG is now showing sinus rhythm in presence of stable patient condition.
Transport decision is to proceed emergently to the hospital due to unstable vital signs.
End-tidal CO2 is at 33 with normal waveform detected.
Keep an eye on respiration, it's steady at 16. 65. Reassessments; retake vital signs: pulse; Partner: The pulse increased and is now at 96.
Blood pressure has improved to 116 over 74.
The stroke scale remains negative for now.
Patient is successfully and securely fastened to the stretcher with a 5-point harness.
No other interventions have been performed as of now.
The final healthcare destination is Chippenham Hospital Emergency Department.
Protocol 3-13 will conclude now that the patient is en route.
A full report has been provided to the receiving ED nurse along with the vital history.
